metadata.summary: Design GraphQL federation (Apollo Router supergraph, subgraphs, entity resolution) and implement query security (depth/complexity limits, persisted queries, auth) at the GraphQL gateway layer.

content.title: GraphQL Federation Gateway: Apollo Router, Subgraphs, and Query Security
content.summary: GraphQL federation via Apollo Router creates a unified supergraph from independently deployed domain subgraphs. The router handles query planning, entity resolution across subgraphs, auth via JWT/JWKS, query depth and complexity limits, and persisted queries. Federation v2 uses @key, @shareable, @external, @requires directives. Never accept a supergraph without running rover supergraph compose to verify composition.

why.p: Federation solves the problem of multiple independent GraphQL services that need to appear as a single unified API. Without federation, either a monolithic GraphQL schema becomes a shared ownership nightmare, or clients must make multiple GraphQL requests to different services. Federation lets each domain team own their subgraph independently while the router composes them into a single supergraph.

Apollo v1 vs v2 directives (@key, @shareable, @external) are regularly mixed up by agents; generated supergraphs fail composition checks. Never accept a supergraph without running rover supergraph compose --config to verify before deployment.

list.item: Multiple teams maintaining independent GraphQL services that need a unified client API.
Migrating from REST to GraphQL across multiple services without a big-bang rewrite.
Building a composite data layer where entities (User, Product, Order) are owned by different services but cross-referenced in queries.
Need GraphQL-specific query controls (depth limits, complexity limits, persisted queries) at a single gateway layer.
Replacing schema stitching with the modern federation approach.
Single-team GraphQL monolith — federation adds operational overhead (multiple services, rover tooling, supergraph composition CI) without benefit when one team owns the whole schema.
REST-only backends where GraphQL is not used — use a REST gateway pattern instead.
Simple BFF patterns where one gateway aggregates a few REST endpoints — REST aggregation in the gateway is simpler than standing up a federation stack.
Teams without GraphQL expertise — federation v2 directives and composition rules have a significant learning curve; invest in training before adopting.
Router (Apollo Router) — single entry point, query planning, query execution across subgraphs, entity resolution, auth, telemetry.
Subgraph — independent GraphQL service for a domain (Users, Products, Orders, Reviews). Each team deploys their subgraph independently.
Supergraph — composed schema from all subgraphs. Generated by rover supergraph compose. The router loads the supergraph schema.
Entity Resolution — cross-subgraph data fetching via @key directive and __resolveReference resolver.

example.title: Federation topology
example.code: Client --> Apollo Router (Gateway) | +----------+----------+----------+ | | | | v v v v Users Orders Products Reviews Subgraph Subgraph Subgraph Subgraph (Team A) (Team B) (Team C) (Team D)

section.name: federation-vs-stitching
section.p: Federation v2 vs schema stitching comparison:

list.item: Federation — subgraphs own their types; each team deploys independently; simpler subgraph development; Apollo Federation v2 (current standard).
Stitching — central gateway owns schema; requires central coordination for cross-service type extensions; gateway becomes complex; legacy approach, avoid for new projects.

rule.statement: Use Apollo Federation v2 for new projects. Never use schema stitching for new federation work — it places all schema ownership at the gateway, creating a bottleneck and coordination overhead for every schema change.
rule.rationale: Federation v2 allows subgraph teams to add and modify types independently. Stitching requires gateway team involvement for every cross-service type extension.

section.name: subgraph-design
section.p: Subgraph design with Federation v2 directives:

rule.statement: Define entities with @key(fields: "id") on the primary owning subgraph. Implement __resolveReference to fetch an entity by its key fields. Use @external on fields defined in another subgraph that are referenced via @requires. Use @shareable when a type or field is intentionally defined in multiple subgraphs.
rule.rationale: Missing @key or incorrect __resolveReference implementations are the most common federation composition failures. Agents mix v1 and v2 directives — always validate with rover.

example.title: Users subgraph entity definition (Federation v2)
Products subgraph extending User entity
Mixing Federation v1 and v2 directives
example.note: Always use extend schema @link with the federation v2 spec URL. Run rover supergraph compose before deploying.

example.title: Apollo Router production config
example.code:
supergraph: introspection: false # disable in production listen: 0.0.0.0:4000
sandbox: enabled: false
cors: origins: - https://app.example.com - https://admin.example.com allow_credentials: true
headers: all: request: - propagate: matching: "x-.*" - insert: name: "x-gateway-timestamp" value: "{{ now }}"
override_subgraph_url: users: http://users-subgraph:4001/graphql products: http://products-subgraph:4002/graphql orders: http://orders-subgraph:4003/graphql reviews: http://reviews-subgraph:4004/graphql
# Query security limits limits: max_depth: 15 max_height: 200 max_aliases: 30 max_root_fields: 20
# Persisted queries (optional but recommended) apq: enabled: true
# Entity cache preview_entity_cache: enabled: true redis: urls: - redis://redis:6379 ttl: 300s
# Per-subgraph timeouts traffic_shaping: all: timeout: 30s subgraphs: users: timeout: 10s products: timeout: 15s orders: timeout: 20s
# JWT authentication authentication: router: jwt: jwks: - url: https://auth.example.com/.well-known/jwks.json header_name: Authorization header_value_prefix: Bearer
# Telemetry telemetry: instrumentation: spans: mode: spec_compliant instruments: default_requirement_level: recommended exporters: tracing: otlp: enabled: true endpoint: http://otel-collector:4317 protocol: grpc metrics: prometheus: enabled: true listen: 0.0.0.0:9090 path: /metrics

section.name: query-security
section.p: GraphQL-specific security at the router layer:

rule.statement: Configure query depth limit (max_depth: 15), complexity limit, alias limit (max_aliases: 30), and root field limit (max_root_fields: 20). Disable introspection in production. Use persisted queries (APQ or safelisting) to prevent arbitrary query execution against production APIs.
rule.rationale: Without depth limits, a malicious client can craft deeply nested queries that force the router to fetch exponentially more data. Without introspection disabled, the full schema is exposed as an attack surface map.

rule.statement: Block mutations for anonymous (unauthenticated) users at the router level. Rate limit by query complexity, not just request count — a query with max_depth=15 touching 4 subgraphs costs more than a simple lookup.
rule.rationale: Anonymous mutations are an obvious abuse vector. Complexity-based rate limiting prevents abuse by clients that craft expensive queries to stay under simple request-count limits.

rule.statement: Run rover supergraph compose --config supergraph.yaml in CI on every subgraph schema change. Never deploy a supergraph composed locally without CI validation. Run rover subgraph check on every subgraph PR to detect breaking changes before merge.
rule.rationale: Composition failures surface at deploy time without CI checks, causing outages. Subgraph check catches breaking changes (removing fields, changing types) that would break existing clients.

example.title: rover supergraph compose workflow
example.code:
# supergraph.yaml — composition config federation_version: =2.3.0 subgraphs: users: routing_url: http://users-subgraph:4001/graphql schema: subgraph_url: http://users-subgraph:4001/graphql products: routing_url: http://products-subgraph:4002/graphql schema: subgraph_url: http://products-subgraph:4002/graphql orders: routing_url: http://orders-subgraph:4003/graphql schema: subgraph_url: http://orders-subgraph:4003/graphql
# Run in CI: rover supergraph compose --config supergraph.yaml > supergraph.graphql
# Check subgraph for breaking changes before merge: rover subgraph check my-graph@prod \ --name users \ --schema users-subgraph/schema.graphql

antipattern.description: Accepting a supergraph without running rover supergraph compose to verify composition.
antipattern.reason: LLMs miss @inaccessible and @override directives; federation v1 and v2 directives get mixed. Composition failures only appear at router startup, causing production outages.

antipattern.description: Mixing Apollo Federation v1 and v2 directives in subgraph schemas.
antipattern.reason: v1: @extends type, @external on key field; v2: extend schema @link, @key without @external on owned fields. Mixed schemas fail composition silently in some cases and loudly in others.

antipattern.description: No query depth or complexity limits in production.
antipattern.reason: Arbitrary deeply nested queries (depth 50+) can trigger cartesian product data fetches across subgraphs, saturating the router and all subgraph services.
Exposes the full schema as an attack surface map. Disable introspection; provide schema documentation via Apollo Studio or Redoc instead.

checklist.check: Use Federation v2 with extend schema @link declaration in all subgraphs
Define @key entities on primary owning subgraph; implement __resolveReference
Run rover supergraph compose in CI on every schema change
Run rover subgraph check on every subgraph PR to catch breaking changes
Disable introspection in production router config
Set query depth, height, alias, and root field limits
Enable JWT authentication at the router; forward user context headers to subgraphs
Configure per-subgraph timeouts and traffic shaping
Enable APQ (automatic persisted queries) or query safelisting
Set up Prometheus metrics and OTLP tracing exporters
Configure entity caching in Redis for frequently accessed entities

section.name: llm-prompts
section.prompt:
Help me design a federated GraphQL schema.
Domains: | Domain | Entities | Team | |----------|-------------------|--------| | Users | User, Profile | Team A | | Products | Product, Category | Team B | | Orders | Order, OrderItem | Team C | | Reviews | Review, Rating | Team D |
Relationships: - User has many Orders - Order has many Products (through OrderItems) - Product has many Reviews - Review belongs to User and Product
Please provide: 1. Entity definitions with Federation v2 @key directives 2. Type extensions for cross-domain relationships 3. Sample queries showing cross-subgraph data requirements 4. rover supergraph compose config 5. Apollo Router configuration

Generate security configuration for Apollo Router.
Requirements: - Disable introspection in production - Query depth limit: 15 - Query complexity limit: 1000 - Max aliases: 30 - Max root fields: 20 - Rate limit by query complexity - Block mutations for unauthenticated users - JWT validation via JWKS
Authentication: - JWT validation at router - Forward user context (user_id, roles) to subgraphs as headers
Generate complete Apollo Router YAML configuration.
